payload.report_md:
# US Stock Watcher Next-Session Forecast — Monday 7/13

**[Monday 7/13 session outlook] Bullish lean — Strong expansion / bull · composite +37 · confidence 48%**

**🔮 Next-session forecast:** The next U.S. regular session is Monday, July 13, opening at 9:30 AM ET (22:30 KST on 7/13). Markets are currently closed, and everything below is a forward-looking assessment of what to expect when trading resumes.

*Data as of: 2026-07-12T06:16:47 · Data quality: fresh*

## Executive Summary

Heading into Monday's open, US Stock Watcher's composite market-state score stands at +37 out of 100, placing conditions in "Strong expansion / bull" territory with a confidence reading of 48% across four measured components. Liquidity, Credit, Earnings, Macro surprises, Valuation, and Positioning were not measured this cycle and have been reweighted out of the composite. With that caveat on breadth of coverage, the setup into Monday's session leans constructive.

## Market Diagnosis

**Indices.** Friday's close leaves major benchmarks positioned as follows ahead of Monday: the S&P 500 finished at 7,575.39, up 0.42% on the day; the Nasdaq-100 closed at 29,825.11, up 0.33%; the Dow ended at 52,637.01, up 0.29%; and the Russell 2000 lagged, slipping 0.49% to 2,977.81 — a divergence worth watching as small caps underperform their large-cap peers heading into the new week.

**Market drivers.** Four factors are currently in play as inputs to the Monday outlook: volatility and index trend both register as supportive, and the yield curve is likewise supportive of the bullish lean. Working against this, the US dollar stands as a headwind.

**Breadth & concentration.** The overall market-state score of +37/100 corresponds to a "Strong expansion / bull" reading, carried with 48% confidence on the strength of four measured components. As noted above, Liquidity, Credit, Earnings, Macro surprises, Valuation, and Positioning remain unmeasured and have been reweighted out of this calculation — a gap that should temper conviction even as the headline signal skews bullish.

**Sector & rotation.** Leadership into Monday is concentrated in Financials, Health Care, and Industrials, while Consumer Staples, Real Estate, and Energy are lagging — a rotation pattern consistent with a risk-on, cyclically-tilted market state.

**Rates, dollar & commodities.** The 10-year Treasury yield sits at 4.54%. The dollar index (DXY) is at 100.97, up a marginal 0.03% on the day. In commodities, WTI crude trades at 71.41, down 0.93%, while gold sits at 4,113.70, down 0.41%.

## Outlook

**Short term.** Framed specifically for Monday's session, the near-term bias is constructive, consistent with the "Strong expansion / bull" market-state reading and its 48% confidence level. This view is conditional, however: should the overall composite score cross back below zero, the constructive lean would flip.

**Medium term.** The medium-term outlook carries the same constructive bias, again anchored to the "Strong expansion / bull" state at 48% confidence, and is subject to the identical flip condition — a move of the overall score below zero would invalidate the bullish tilt.

**Medium-to-long term.** Extending further out, the medium-to-long-term view remains constructive on the same basis — the "Strong expansion / bull" market state at 48% confidence — with the same caveat that a break below zero on the overall score would reverse the stance.

## Top News

Heading into the weekend, headline flow relevant to Monday's session includes: memory giants racing to expand AI chip capacity (*The Korea Herald*); gold turning bearish amid the US-Iran conflict as Fed rate-hike fears slam bullion, with the Bank of Taiwan warning of a potential test of $4,000 (*finance.biggo.com*); a "Market Ready to Go?" screen highlighting five stocks near buy points with big earnings looming (*Investor's Business Daily*); coverage of US regional bank stocks that retail investors are watching as Treasury yields rise (*simplywall.st*); a hawkish-turn read on the Fed's July 2026 interest rate outlook amid an inflation surge (*Intellectia AI*); and a piece noting that, while everyone owns VOO, an overlooked S&P 500 ETF is somehow cheaper (*AOL.com*).
